does: This paper introduces a novel command-and-control architecture built on the Model Context Protocol to orchestrate distributed, adaptive reconnaissance agents for autonomous red teaming operations. The work systematically evaluates existing generative AI approaches for cybersecurity tasks, from specialized fine-tuned models to modular agentic frameworks, analyzing their effectiveness across automation capabilities and task-specific accuracy. The key contribution is demonstrating that MCP-based C2 coordination not only improves goal-directed behavior of multi-agent systems but also eliminates detectable host and network artifacts traditionally associated with command-and-control behavior, making the agents operate more covertly across networked environments. The paper presents both the architectural design and empirical findings showing how asynchronous coordination mechanisms reduce forensic signatures.
connects: This work builds on a growing literature in AI-powered cybersecurity automation that has explored how large language models and generative agents can conduct reconnaissance, vulnerability discovery, and penetration testing tasks. It extends prior research on specialized versus generalist approaches to red teaming by proposing a more sophisticated coordination mechanism using MCP, which enables distributed agents to share context and tools. The paper responds to documented limitations in existing agentic frameworks—hallucinations, context window constraints, and difficulty maintaining coherent long-horizon behavior—that have been identified in recent work on LLM-based agents. It also engages with emerging concerns about detection and attribution of automated attacks, building on foundational work in adversarial operations and stealth techniques.
enables: This architecture enables more sophisticated and stealthy autonomous offensive operations, pointing toward future research on adversarial agent coordination, multi-agent system design for security, and the arms race between defensive detection and offensive evasion. The work highlights how protocol abstractions like MCP can be repurposed to enhance covertness while improving coordination, suggesting pathways for studying the interplay between capability and detectability in agentic systems. It raises important questions about the future of red teaming as a practice and the need for complementary defensive mechanisms capable of detecting or attributing behavior from distributed, protocol-abstracted agent networks. This could motivate research into detection methods for stealth command-and-control, provenance tracking in agentic systems, and ethical frameworks for autonomous offensive security research.
